timeline.text: Data-quality alarm — stg_orders: unique(order_id) failing; yesterday's revenue reads 2× and the CFO noticed before we did

timeline.cmd:
SELECT order_id, COUNT(*) AS copies FROM stg_orders
GROUP BY order_id HAVING copies > 1 LIMIT 5;

SELECT load_batch, COUNT(*) AS rows_loaded FROM orders_raw
GROUP BY load_batch ORDER BY load_batch DESC LIMIT 6;
timeline.note: the batch ledger never lies — was something loaded twice?

timeline.cause: Yesterday's file was loaded twice — the loader retried after a timeout that had actually succeeded, so every order from that day exists under two batch tags and revenue reads exactly 2×. The orders are real; the second copy is not.
receipts: failed check: unique(order_id) on stg_orders — 111 duplicated order_id values
alternative untested: upstream source emitted duplicate order IDs

timeline.explain: Compute the expected duplicate count inside the transaction, collect only the retry batch's matching rowids, and snapshot those exact rows. The run commits only because the candidate-row check matched the expected 111 duplicates and the final check returned zero remaining cross-batch duplicates; either failed gate executes INSERT OR ROLLBACK before COMMIT.
timeline.rollback: BEGIN IMMEDIATE; INSERT INTO orders_raw SELECT * FROM orders_raw_restore_20260710_02; SELECT changes() AS restored_rows; DROP TABLE orders_raw_restore_20260710_02; COMMIT; -- restores only deleted rows into the original schema
timeline.followup: Make the loader idempotent — stage into a temp table and MERGE on order_id — so a retry can never double-load again.

timeline.text: All tasks rebuilt, every check green — marts are fresh and the numbers reconcile. Standing down.

title: revenue doubled overnight — yesterday loaded twice
